planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in virtually any soil in shade; Abraham-Isaac-Jacob is extraordinarily adaptable and will succeed in dry shade, wet shade, heavy clay, or sandy soil — conditions that defeat most shade groundcovers. It is slow to establish in the first year but spreads reliably thereafter by underground rhizomes.

directions: Plant in virtually any moist to dry soil in full to part shade; Abraham-Isaac-Jacob is a robust, large-leafed borage-family perennial from Turkey and the Caucasus that has earned a dedicated following among shade gardeners for one exceptional quality: it will cover dry shade under large deciduous trees where almost nothing else grows, spreading steadily by rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat of enormous, rough-textured, heart-shaped leaves reaching 12–18 inches across on mature plants. In very early spring — February or March — clusters of small, intensely blue, reflexed flowers emerge on bare stems before the foliage appears, giving the unusual appearance of flowers seemingly floating above bare ground. The common name 'Abraham-Isaac-Jacob' describes the three-color pattern of the buds (purple), open flowers (blue), and spent flowers (pink), all visible simultaneously on the nodding flower cluster, similar to Pulmonaria. After flowering, the bold, tropical-looking foliage expands to dominate and cool the ground beneath trees for the rest of the season. An outstanding solution for the most difficult dry-shade situations.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring in moist, well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to part shade; yellow patrinia self-sows moderately once established, maintaining a colony without becoming invasive. Established plants develop a deep taproot and should not be moved; site carefully from the start.

directions: Sow seeds in moist, well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; yellow patrinia is a Japanese and Korean perennial that has become a valued late-season component in naturalistic perennial designs, producing airy, branching sprays of small, bright-yellow flowers from July through September — an unusually long bloom period — carried on slender, branching stems above deeply cut, dark-green basal foliage. The finely divided flower clusters create a golden haze effect when massed and combine beautifully with blue salvias, purple asters, ornamental grasses, and late-season rudbeckias. As autumn proceeds, the yellow flowers gradually age to a warm bronze-orange and are joined by ornamental seed heads, extending the season further. Yellow patrinia is a significant and long-lasting cut flower, popular in Japanese ikebana arrangements and increasingly in Western floral design. The plant self-sows reliably without becoming weedy and is attractive to wasps, small bees, and butterflies. It is long-lived once established and thrives in a remarkably wide range of conditions.

planting.note: Divide tuberous roots in early spring or fall, or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade; Azure monkshood resents dry conditions and performs best in cool, moist positions with partial shade from afternoon sun. Do not move established plants unnecessarily — they resent disturbance. ALL PARTS ARE EXTREMELY TOXIC if ingested or if sap contacts broken skin.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; azure monkshood is the most valuable of the autumn-blooming monkshoods, native to central China and blooming in September and October when deep blue flowers are precious — its large, dense spikes of rich violet-blue, hooded flowers ('monks' hoods') are among the last major perennial flowers of the season, providing an anchor of rich cool color in the autumn garden when most perennials have finished. The flowers are architecturally bold and distinctive in shape — each with the characteristic helmet-shaped hood that protects the nectar and is adapted for pollination by bumblebees, the only insects strong enough to push into the hooded flower. The deep, glossy, dark-green, deeply lobed foliage is attractive throughout the growing season before the late bloom. The cultivar 'Arendsii' produces particularly large, luminous dark-blue flowers. EXTREME CAUTION: all parts of all monkshoods contain aconitine, one of the most potent plant toxins known — wear gloves when handling and keep away from children and pets.

planting.note: Sow seeds indoors in late winter at cool temperatures (50–60°F) without heat; Himalayan blue poppy requires cool germination temperatures and is killed by heat above 75°F during germination. In the first year, remove any flower buds that form (preventing bloom) to allow the plant to build root strength — this greatly increases perennial persistence. It performs as a monocarpic perennial in hot climates, living 2–3 years.

directions: Start seeds at cool temperatures indoors and grow in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in part shade; Himalayan blue poppy produces flowers of a blue so pure and saturated that they are difficult to believe are real — large, clear sky-blue, silky-petaled flowers 3–4 inches across with golden stamens, carried in loose clusters on hairy stems in early summer. Native to the alpine meadows and open forests of Tibet, Bhutan, and Yunnan at 10,000–16,000 feet elevation, it requires cool summer temperatures to thrive and does not perform well in hot, humid climates. In the Pacific Northwest, Scotland, and the cooler parts of the northeastern US, it is a long-lived perennial; in hot-summer climates it tends toward monocarpic behavior — flowering spectacularly and declining. The key to perennial persistence is removing flower buds in year one to allow full root development. Requires consistently moist, cool, acidic conditions with afternoon shade. For many plantspeople, successfully establishing and flowering this plant is considered an achievement; for others in suitable climates it is surprisingly manageable.

planting.note: Direct-sow or allow to self-sow in almost any well-drained soil including wall crevices, paving gaps, and gravel; Mexican fleabane is a prolific self-seeder that naturalizes readily and is best established by simply allowing seeds to find their own level in the garden. It is short-lived as an individual plant (2–3 years) but self-sows perpetually, filling gaps and softening edges throughout the garden.

directions: Sow in almost any well-drained soil, including crevices in walls and paving, in full sun; Mexican fleabane is one of the most charming and generous self-seeding plants for temperate gardens, producing a near-continuous display of small daisy flowers from late spring through autumn — the flowers open white, then age through blush-pink to deep rose over the course of several days, so a flowering plant simultaneously carries white, pink, and rose flowers creating a multicolor cottage-garden effect. The plants themselves are small and airy, with tiny, narrow leaves, creating fine-textured mounds that tuck into gaps in walls, cascade over stone edges, spill from urns, and fill paving cracks — places where larger plants cannot grow. The combination of extremely long bloom, color change, minimal size, and willingness to grow in dry, lean conditions in full sun makes this one of the most useful 'spontaneous' plants in the garden. Originally from Mexico and Central America, it has naturalized widely in the Mediterranean, parts of Australia, and coastal California and Pacific Northwest in the US.
